title: Squeezing dynamical singlets in bilayer nickelates
authors: Harrison LaBollita, Andrew J. Millis, Olivier Gingras
abstract: We present realistic calculations within the density functional plus cluster dynamical mean-field formalism indicating that the physics of the the bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates is to a significant extent controlled by interlayer "dynamical singlets'' which are formed from the $3z^{2}-r^{2}$ orbitals singly occupied by electrons and are hybridized with itinerant planar $x^{2}-y^{2}$ orbitals. The hybridization is found to respond differently to hydrostatic pressure and to epitaxial strain, capturing the experimentally observed dichotomy between bulk single crystals and epitaxial thin films and reproducing several experimental results including angle-resolved photoemission and transport measurements.
summary: This work uses advanced computational methods, specifically CDMFT, to model bilayer nickelates under external strain and pressure. It reveals that structural changes tune the system's physics by controlling interlayer 'dynamical singlets.' The findings successfully explain experimental dichotomies in transport properties and provide insights into the mechanism driving high-temperature superconductivity.
main_problem: The study aims to understand how structural changes, specifically hydrostatic pressure and epitaxial strain, tune the normal state electronic structure of bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates.
main_result: The physics is governed by interlayer 'dynamical singlets' formed from $3z^2-r^2$ orbitals hybridized with planar $x^2-y^2$ orbitals, and pressure/strain induce distinct electronic responses (e.g., T-linear vs. T^2 resistivity).
method: The authors employ Cluster Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (CDMFT) coupled with DFT calculations to model the correlated electron behavior.
model_or_system: The system is bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates ($	ext{La}_3	ext{Ni}_2	ext{O}_7$), modeled using a four-orbital Wannier representation for Ni-$e_g$ orbitals, incorporating Hubbard-Kanamori interactions.
key_observables: Low-energy spectral function $A(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$, scattering rate $\Gamma(T)$, mass renormalization ($m^*/m_{	ext{band}}$), and orbital occupation probabilities $P(N)$.
important_parameters: Hydrostatic pressure (up to 30 GPa) and epitaxial strain (e.g., $-2\%$), Hubbard interaction parameters ($U, U', J$).
main_assumptions: The low-energy model is restricted to four $e_g$ orbitals; calculations are performed using the bonding-antibonding basis.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spectral functions and Fermi surfaces under varying strain/pressure; other figures show temperature dependence of scattering rates ($\Gamma$) and orbital-resolved self-energies ($\Sigma(\omega)$) for both structural perturbations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically maps the effect of external structural tuning (pressure vs. strain) on the correlated electronic structure, contrasting results from CDMFT against experimental observations like ARPES and transport measurements.
why_interesting: The detailed analysis of orbital-selective correlations and the emergence of dynamical singlets provide a rich theoretical framework for understanding unconventional superconductivity mechanisms in strongly correlated oxides.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Low-energy many-body electronic structure of La3Ni2O7 under hydrostatic pressure and compressive epitaxial strain. (a) Schematic of the bilayer cluster quantum impurity used in CDMFT, indicating local (Σll) and interlayer (Σ12) self-energies. Ni and O atoms are shown as blue and red spheres, respectively; overlapping oxygen positions indicate the structural distortions induced by the external structural perturbations. (b) Fermi surface (kz = 0) of the ambient pressure Wannier Hamiltonian with the orbital character projected onto each sheet: x2 −y2 (blue), 3z2 −r2 (green), and mixed (purple). (c-h) CDMFT electronic structure at T = 1/40 (∼290 K) for (c,f) ambient pressure, (d,g) −2%...

FIG. 2. Electron-electron scattering and low-energy spectrum of La3Ni2O7 under hydrostatic pressure (30 GPa) and −2% compressive strain. (a) Temperature dependence of the scat- tering rate Γ ∝−Tr[ImΣ(iωn →0)]. Dashed lines corre- spond to fits with forms f(T) = a + bT 2 and f(T) = a + bT for the strained and pressure case, respectively. (b) Local spectral function for the z orbitals, Az(ω), at T = 1/100 eV (≃116 K) for 30 GPa (green) and −2% compressive epitaxial strain (purple).

FIG. 4. Effect of pressure on the low-energy effective in-plane (˜t∥) and out-of-plane (˜t⊥) hoppings of the bilayer RP nicke- lates. Definitions in the main text. The values are expressed as percent change from the ambient pressure calculation. The marker color corresponds to the experimentally observed su- perconducting transition temperature (T exp c ) shown on the colorbar.

FIG. 3. Signatures of dynamical singlets in La3Ni2O7. (a) Valence histograms of the four orbital quantum impurity problem for different total charge configurations (N). For each case (colorful dot), we distinguish with blue color the configurations which have two electrons in the z orbitals (z2) and N −2 electrons in the x orbitals (xN−2) while all other configurations are denoted in grey. Inset: Expectation values for ⟨nz 1↑nz 1↓⟩(double occupancy) and ⟨nz 1↑nz 2↓⟩(one z electron on each site with opposite spins.).
